note:
γ batch input. 100 clusters.

For each cluster, output a verdict in strict JSON:
  {"cluster_hash": "<hash>", "word": "<word>", "decision": "merge|split|unsure", "confidence": 0.0-1.0, "reasoning": "1 sentence", "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100, "merged_text": "..." | null}

Save as: data/simplify_diff/gamma_batch_verdicts.json

GAMMA_SYSTEM_PROMPT:
You are an English vocabulary teacher reviewing 2-3 senses of a single word for an IELTS learner's flashcard.

YOUR RULE (the user's "80-90% rule"):
Merge the senses ONLY IF a single definition lets the learner understand AT LEAST 80-90% of all examples across the senses. Otherwise SPLIT.

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict JSON, no prose):
{
  "decision": "merge" | "split" | "unsure",
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0,
  "reasoning": "1 sentence",
  "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100,
  "merged_text": "single definition" | null
}

DECISION RULES:
- merge:    1 def covers >=80% examples, learner doesn't need separate cards
- split:    senses are too distinct, need 2+ cards
- unsure:   borderline 70-80%, default to SPLIT in production (conservative)

NEVER merge senses that are clearly different POS, register, or context (e.g. "go" verb vs "go" noun).

senses.text: a time of great danger, difficulty or doubt when problems must be solved or important decisions must be made
examples: an economic/a financial crisis
The government is attempting to solve the debt crisis through spending cuts.
The government is facing a political crisis.
a humanitarian crisis created by war and drought
a housing/an energy crisis

senses.text: a time when a problem, a bad situation or an illness is at its worst point
examples: Their marriage has reached crisis point.
The fever has passed its crisis.
The team's dismal season has reached crisis point.

senses.text: a strong feeling that something/somebody exists or is true; confidence that something/somebody is good or right
examples: I admire his passionate belief in what he is doing.
belief in God/democracy
The incident has shaken my belief (= made me have less confidence) in the police.
They share a belief that there is life after death.

senses.text: an opinion about something; something that you think is true
examples: There is a general belief that things will soon get better.
There is a widespread belief that multinationals have too much power.
a widely/commonly held belief
She acted in the belief that she was doing good.
I took the job in the mistaken belief that I would be able to stay in London.

senses.text: something that you believe, especially as part of your religion
examples: religious/political beliefs
A society should be judged on its beliefs and values.
Some people hold beliefs about the world that are not supported by science.

senses.text: very sad and without hope
examples: You mustn't let yourself get depressed.
She felt very depressed about the future.
He was really depressed at the thought of going into the office.
She was depressed over the recent death of her mother.
I was depressed by our lack of progress.

senses.text: affected by the medical condition of depression
examples: She became severely depressed.
There is medical evidence to show that he is clinically depressed.
She suffers from sleep disturbance and depressed mood.

senses.text: to try and give an answer or make a judgement about something without being sure of all the facts
examples: I don't really know. I'm just guessing.
We can only guess at her reasons for leaving.
He guessed right/wrong.
I'd guess that she's about 30.
Can you guess where I've been?

senses.text: to find the right answer to a question or the truth without knowing all the facts
examples: She guessed the answer straight away.
You'll never guess what she told me.
You would never guess (that) she had problems. She’s always so cheerful.
So it was Rob who broke the window? I might have guessed!
If I had to guess, I'd say she was the one responsible for this.

senses.text: used to show that you are going to say something surprising or exciting
examples: Guess what! He's asked me out!
Guess who I've just seen!

senses.text: the activity of broadcasting programmes for people to listen to; the programmes that are broadcast
examples: The play was written specially for radio.
I listen to the radio on the way to work.
The interview was broadcast on radio and television.
Did you hear the interview with him on the radio?
national radio

senses.text: a piece of equipment used for listening to programmes that are broadcast to the public
examples: to turn the radio on/off
to have the radio on
a car radio
His radio is permanently tuned to Heart FM.
He drove along with his windows open and the radio blaring out.

senses.text: the process of sending and receiving messages through the air using electromagnetic waves
examples: He was unable to contact Blake by radio.
to keep in radio contact
radio frequencies/signals/communications
It was fascinating to listen to the radio chatter from the ships.
The message was sent by radio.

senses.text: a piece of equipment, for example on ships or planes, for sending and receiving radio signals
examples: They heard the gale warning over the ship’s radio.
The airline company failed to reach the plane on its radio.
There were calls for cab radios to link train drivers and signal boxes.

senses.text: to put your foot down while you are stepping or walking
examples: Ouch! You trod on my toe!
Careful you don't tread in that puddle.

senses.text: to move, push or press something with your feet
examples: Don't tread ash into the carpet!
The wine is still made by treading grapes in the traditional way.
She planted the seeds and trod the earth down.

senses.text: to walk somewhere
examples: Few people had trod this path before.
He was treading quietly and cautiously.

senses.text: the act of saving somebody/something from a dangerous or difficult situation; the fact of being saved
examples: We had given up hope of rescue.
A wealthy benefactor came to their rescue with a generous donation.
a rescue operation/mission
a mountain rescue team
rescue helicopters/boats/workers

senses.text: an occasion when somebody/something is saved from a dangerous or difficult situation
examples: Ten fishermen were saved in a daring sea rescue.
Her own boat capsized after she attempted a rescue.

senses.text: to continue to exist for longer than expected
examples: The faint smell of her perfume lingered in the room.
The civil war lingered on well into the 1930s.

senses.text: to stay somewhere for longer because you do not want to leave; to spend a long time doing something
examples: She lingered for a few minutes to talk to Nick.
We lingered over breakfast on the terrace.

senses.text: the process of doing something in order to make something happen or to deal with a situation
examples: The time has come for action if these beautiful animals are to survive.
to threaten/demand/call for action
Firefighters took action immediately to stop the blaze spreading.
Divers were close at hand, ready to go into action if the stunt went wrong.
What is the best course of action in the circumstances?

senses.text: a thing that somebody does
examples: Each of us must take responsibility for our own actions.
They sought to justify their actions.

senses.text: the dark shape that somebody/something’s form makes on a surface, for example on the ground, when they are between the light and the surface
examples: The children were having fun, chasing each other's shadows.
As the curtains moved in the breeze, the shadows on the floor seemed to dance.
The ship's sail cast a shadow on the water.
The shadows lengthened as the sun went down.
The man's face is hidden in a deep shadow cast by his umbrella.

senses.text: darkness in a place or on something, especially so that you cannot easily see who or what is there
examples: I thought I saw a figure standing in the shadows.
His face was deep in shadow, turned away from her.
A man in black emerged from the shadows.
It had grown dark, and the shadows could be hiding anything.
It's a spooky story about the monsters that lurk in the shadows.

senses.text: the quality of being constant in your support of somebody/something
examples: Can I count on your loyalty?
They swore their loyalty to the king.

senses.text: a strong feeling that you want to support and be loyal to somebody/something
examples: a case of divided loyalties (= with strong feelings of support for two different causes, people, etc.)

senses.text: the fact of being in a particular place
examples: He hardly seemed to notice my presence.
Her presence during the crisis had a calming effect.
Your presence is requested at the meeting.
They married in the presence of their family members.

senses.text: the fact of being in a particular place or thing
examples: The test can identify the presence of abnormalities in the unborn child.
One of the first things I note when I walk into a client's home is the presence or absence of pictures around the room.
The company now has a strong presence in Germany.

senses.text: connected with the way in which a business, machine, system, etc. works
examples: operational activities/costs/difficulties

senses.text: ready or available to be used
examples: The new airport is now fully operational.

senses.text: the roasted seeds (called coffee beans) of a tropical bush; a powder made from them
examples: ground/real/instant coffee
decaffeinated coffee
a jar of coffee
a blend of Brazilian and Colombian coffees
a coffee plantation

senses.text: a hot drink made from coffee powder and boiling water. It may be drunk with milk and/or sugar added.
examples: to drink/sip coffee
Do you want to have coffee with me sometime?
black/white coffee (= without/with milk)
a cup of coffee
a coffee cup/mug

senses.text: a cup of coffee
examples: Do you want to go and get a coffee?
Two strong black coffees, please.

senses.text: doing something as a paid job rather than as a hobby
examples: professional athletes/players
a professional golfer
She began her professional career as a dancer 20 years ago.
After he won the amateur championship he turned professional.
I enjoyed boxing but I was never good enough to go professional.

senses.text: done as a paid job rather than as a hobby
examples: In professional sport winning is everything.
the world of professional football

senses.text: the clothes worn by people from a particular place or during a particular historical period
examples: an exhibition of Victorian costumes
She wore a traditional costume of bright pink silk.
a painting of a woman in Welsh costume
Many locals dressed in period costume (= typical of a particular time in history) for the celebrations.

senses.text: the clothes worn by actors in a play or film, or worn by somebody to make them look like somebody/something else
examples: The former dancer has also designed the costumes.
The costumes for this production were gorgeous.
They are already planning their costumes for Halloween.
He went to the party in a giant chicken costume.
The actors were still in costume and make-up.

senses.text: a large area of land, usually without exact limits or borders
examples: mountainous/coastal regions
the northern/southern/central, etc. region
This is one of the most densely populated regions of North America.
There are more than two million people living in the region.
Many new species have been discovered in the Amazon region.

senses.text: one of the areas that a country is divided into, that has its own customs and/or its own government
examples: Bilbao is the largest city in the Basque region in northern Spain.
The country is divided into 17 autonomous regions.

senses.text: the work that is your job
examples: Report for duty at 8 a.m.
He was the duty manager that day.

senses.text: tasks that are part of your job
examples: I spend a lot of time on administrative duties.
The princess has taken on her mother's official duties.
to perform/fulfil/discharge your duties
Your duties will include greeting visitors and answering the phone.

senses.text: if you nod, nod your head or your head nods, you move your head up and down to show agreement, understanding, etc.
examples: I asked him if he would help me and he nodded.
‘Ready?’ She nodded at me and we set off.
Her head nodded in agreement.
He nodded his head sympathetically.
She nodded approval.

senses.text: to move your head down and up once to say hello or goodbye to somebody or to give them a sign to do something
examples: The president nodded to the crowd as he passed in the motorcade.
She nodded at him to begin speaking
to nod a greeting

senses.text: to move your head in the direction of somebody/something to show that you are talking about them/it
examples: I asked where Steve was and she nodded in the direction of the kitchen.
She nodded towards his suitcase. ‘Going somewhere?’ she asked.

senses.text: a measurement of the speed at which something happens
examples: Figures published today show another fall in the rate of inflation.
Most people walk at an average rate of five kilometres an hour.
The number of reported crimes is increasing at an alarming rate.
At the rate you work, you'll never finish!

senses.text: a measurement of the number of times something happens or something does something during a particular period
examples: We have seen a reduction in the crime rate over the last 12 months.
a high success/failure rate
the youth unemployment rate
His pulse rate dropped suddenly.
a high/low/rising rate of unemployment

senses.text: to give something to somebody or allow them to use something that belongs to you, which they have to return to you later
examples: They will lend equipment free of charge.
I've lent the car to a friend.
The gallery has been willing to lend out works of art in the past.
The charity lends out laptops to support learning at home.
Can you lend me £10?

senses.text: to give money to somebody on condition that they pay it back over a period of time and pay interest on it
examples: The bank lends money at very cheap rates.
They refused to lend us the money.
The bank refused to lend the money to us.
Banks are less willing to lend in these uncertain times.
There are a large number of financial providers lending to businesses across the country.

senses.text: the way in which two people, groups or countries behave towards each other or deal with each other
examples: a personal/working relationship
a master-servant relationship
This is an opportunity to build positive customer relationships.
The relationship between the police and the local community has improved.
the bilateral relationship between the US and China

senses.text: a loving and/or sexual friendship between two people
examples: Their affair did not develop into a long-term relationship.
It was his first sexual relationship.
Money problems have put a strain on their relationship.
She was having a relationship with a younger man.
She got involved in a romantic relationship with a colleague.

senses.text: correct for a particular situation or thing, or for a particular person
examples: Have you got the right money (= the exact amount) for the bus fare?
What's the right way to do this?
You're not holding it the right way up.
Are you sure you've got that on the right way round?
This is a step in the right direction.

senses.text: in a normal or good enough condition
examples: I don't feel quite right today (= I feel ill).
Things aren't right between her parents.
If only I could have helped put matters right.
That sausage doesn't smell right.
The meat doesn't taste right to me.

senses.text: the act of inviting somebody or of being invited
examples: He produced an official letter of invitation.
Admission is by invitation only.
A concert was held at the invitation of the mayor.

senses.text: a card or piece of paper that you use to invite somebody to something
examples: We've already sent out the invitations.
Have you ordered the wedding invitations yet?

senses.text: the regular pattern of weather conditions of a particular place
examples: a tropical/warm/mild/temperate/cold climate
the harsh climate of the Arctic regions

senses.text: an area with particular weather conditions
examples: They wanted to move to a warmer climate.
Little grows in such a dry climate.

senses.text: having great power or force; very effective
examples: a powerful engine
a powerful voice
a powerful telescope
A huge and powerful storm is on track to hit Florida.
I traded my computer in for a more powerful model.

senses.text: having a strong effect on your thoughts, feelings or body
examples: a powerful image/symbol/speech
He was prescribed a more powerful painkiller.
Fear is a powerful emotion.
Her background has had a powerful influence on her film-making.
A successful prosecution would send a powerful message.

senses.text: physically strong
examples: a powerful body
a powerful athlete
When she trots she covers ground like a powerful horse rather than a pony.

senses.text: a formal meeting at which somebody is asked questions to see if they are suitable for a particular job, or for a course of study at a college, university, etc.
examples: a job interview
She's been called for (an) interview.
He has an interview next week for the manager's job.
I got an interview with a PR company for a trainee position.
In the interview they asked me about my future plans.

senses.text: a meeting (often a public one) at which a journalist asks somebody questions in order to find out their opinions
examples: a television/TV/radio/newspaper interview
to do/give an interview (= to agree to answer questions)
to conduct an interview (= to ask somebody questions in public)
The interview was published in all the papers.
Yesterday, in an interview on German television, the minister denied the reports.

senses.text: a private meeting between people when questions are asked and answered
examples: a telephone/phone interview
She's had an interview with the careers adviser.
He gave a very full account in long interviews with experienced police officers.
Interviews and questionnaires are the most important tools in market research.

senses.text: connected with people’s feelings (= with the emotions)
examples: emotional problems/stress
The emotional impact of the film is powerful and lasting.
He clearly feels an emotional connection to the place.
a child’s emotional and intellectual development
He tried his best to provide emotional support for them.

senses.text: causing people to feel strong emotions
examples: emotional language
abortion and other emotional issues
The anniversary of the accident was very emotional for him.
It was obviously an emotional moment for everyone involved.
This is a very emotional time for me.

senses.text: showing strong emotions, sometimes in a way that other people think is unnecessary
examples: an emotional response/reaction
She was in a very emotional state.
He tends to get emotional on these occasions.
They made an emotional appeal for help.
Don't be so emotional about everything!

senses.text: a comparison of one thing with another thing that has similar features; a feature that is similar
examples: The teacher drew an analogy between the human heart and a pump.
There are no analogies with any previous legal cases.

senses.text: the process of comparing one thing with another thing that has similar features in order to explain it
examples: learning by analogy
We can understand this theory by analogy with human beings.

senses.text: of the most excellent type or quality
examples: That's the best movie I've ever seen!
He wrote his best songs before he was 25.
She was one of the best tennis players of her generation.
Is that your best suit?
They've been best friends (= closest friends) since they were children.

senses.text: giving most pleasure; happiest
examples: Those were the best years of my life.

senses.text: most suitable or appropriate
examples: What's the best way to cook steak?
Which is the best tool for this job?
The best thing to do would be to apologize.
It's best if you go now.
I'm not in the best position to advise you.

senses.text: a piece of equipment producing a flow of water that you stand under to wash yourself; the small room or part of a room that contains a shower
examples: He's in the shower.
I've just got out of the shower.
an en-suite shower room
a shower cubicle/curtain
an electric shower

senses.text: the act of washing yourself with a shower
examples: a hot/cold shower
to have a shower
to take a shower
shower gel

senses.text: used to describe a situation in which people or organizations compete against each other
examples: competitive games/sports
Graduates have to fight for jobs in an increasingly competitive market.
Those businesses are operating in an extremely competitive environment.
The company has succeeded in a highly competitive industry.

senses.text: trying very hard to be better than others
examples: You have to be highly competitive to do well in sport these days.

senses.text: less hard than average
examples: soft rocks such as limestone
soft corals
soft cheeses
the soft palate

senses.text: smooth and pleasant to touch
examples: soft skin
a dress made from the finest, softest silk
The fabric has a beautifully soft texture.
Clean the lens with a soft cloth.

senses.text: the round object that moves around the earth once every 27½ days and shines at night by light reflected from the sun
examples: the surface of the moon
the first man to walk on the moon
a moon landing

senses.text: the moon as it appears in the sky at a particular time
examples: a crescent moon
There's no moon tonight (= no moon can be seen).
By the light of the moon I could just make out shapes and outlines.

senses.text: connected with the state, government or public affairs
examples: a monarch without political power
He was a political prisoner (= one who was put in prison for holding opinions that the government thought dangerous).
a period of political stability
a political situation/crisis
The police force is facing intense political pressure to reopen the investigation.

senses.text: connected with the different groups working in politics, especially their policies and the competition between them
examples: a political party/leader/opponent
What are your political sympathies?
Peace organizations are being used as fronts for political agendas.
a successful political career
the right to participate in political processes.

senses.text: an amount or a number of something
examples: a large/small quantity of something
vast/huge quantities of food
enormous/massive/considerable/substantial quantities of something
a product that is cheap to produce in large quantities
Lamb is consumed in large quantities in rural areas.

senses.text: the measurement of something by saying how much of it there is
examples: The data is limited in terms of both quality and quantity.

senses.text: to be unable to remember something that has happened in the past or information that you knew in the past
examples: Oh, I almost forgot—there was a call for you.
I'd completely forgotten about the money he owed me.
I never forget a face.
I've forgotten my user name.
Who could forget his speech at last year's party?

senses.text: to deliberately stop thinking about somebody/something
examples: Try to forget about what happened.
Could you possibly forget about work for five minutes?
Forget him!
Let's forget our differences and be friends.
Forget glasses; soon your computer display will correct your vision.

senses.text: a supply of goods that is available for sale in a shop
examples: We have a fast turnover of stock.
That particular model is not currently in stock.
I'm afraid we're temporarily out of stock.
We don't carry a large stock of pine furniture.
It is important to carry out regular stock checks.

senses.text: a supply of something that is available for use
examples: Food stocks are running low.
declining fish stocks in the oceans
a country’s housing stock (= all the houses available for living in)
She's built up a good stock of teaching materials over the years.

senses.text: an official examination of the facts about a situation, crime, etc.
examples: a criminal/murder/police investigation
an accident investigation team
The president has promised a full investigation.
The authorities have launched an investigation to determine why the ferry sank.
She remains under investigation for fraud.

senses.text: a scientific or academic examination of the facts of a subject or problem
examples: Further investigation revealed a flaw in this theory.
Refer patients with these symptoms for further investigation.
the results of a preliminary investigation into stress at work

senses.text: a tool or device used for a particular task, especially for specialist or scientific work
examples: surgical/optical/scientific instruments
Scientists then began using powerful instruments to peer through the planet's dense atmosphere.
syringes, needles or other sharp instruments
medieval instruments of torture such as the rack and the wheel
Instruments for diagnosis and surgery have become hugely more sophisticated.

senses.text: a device used for measuring speed, distance, temperature, etc. in a vehicle or on a machine
examples: data gathered by instruments aboard the satellite
the flight instruments
There was a warning light flashing on the instrument panel.

senses.text: a set of rules or instructions that are given by an official organization telling you how to do something, especially something difficult
examples: The government has drawn up guidelines for schools during a pandemic.

senses.text: something that can be used to help you make a decision or form an opinion
examples: The figures are a useful guideline when buying a house.

senses.text: the unfair treatment of people who belong to a different race; violent behaviour towards them
examples: a victim of racism
ugly outbreaks of racism
the urgent need to address institutional racism within the organization
It is important to identify root causes that perpetuate systemic racism in the city.
structural/casual racism

senses.text: the belief that there are different races of people with different characteristics and abilities, and that some races are better than others; a general belief about a whole group of people based only on their race
examples: irrational racism
Many 19th-century scientific theories were underpinned by racism.

senses.text: that allows information to be passed continuously and in both directions between a computer or other device and the person who uses it
examples: interactive displays/video
using ICT to create an interactive learning experience
an interactive online map that helps people find bicycle routes through the city
A series of interactive exhibits will teach visitors about the history of the bridge.
The program is fully interactive.

senses.text: that involves people working together and having an influence on each other
examples: The school believes in interactive teaching methods.

senses.text: believing that people only do things to help themselves rather than for good or honest reasons
examples: a cynical view/smile
Do you have to be so cynical about everything?

senses.text: not believing that something good will happen or that something is important
examples: I'm a bit cynical about the benefits of the plan.
Her experiences with men have made her thoroughly cynical about love.
I'm a little cynical about her motives.

senses.text: a building where people are kept as a punishment for a crime they have committed, or while they are waiting for trial
examples: She went to prison for tax evasion.
He was sent to prison for five years.
She is in prison, awaiting trial.
Her son is in prison for murder.
He served four years in prison for killing a man.

senses.text: the system of keeping people in prisons
examples: the prison service/system
The government insists that ‘prison works’ and plans to introduce a tougher sentencing policy for people convicted of violent crime.

senses.text: to cover a surface or object with paint
examples: Paint the shed with weather-resistant paint.
a brightly painted barge
a freshly painted fence
We've had the house painted.
The walls were painted yellow.

senses.text: to make a picture or design using paints
examples: to paint portraits/pictures
A friend painted the children for me (= painted a picture of the children).
Slogans had been painted on the walls.
She paints in oils.
a portrait painted in oils

senses.text: the practice of focusing your mind in silence, especially for religious reasons or in order to make your mind calm
examples: She found peace through yoga and meditation.

senses.text: serious thoughts on a particular subject that somebody writes down or speaks
examples: his meditations on life and art

senses.text: to continue to live or exist
examples: She was the last surviving member of the family.
Of the six people injured in the crash, only two survived.
Many of these teachers are struggling to survive financially.
He must betray his friend in order to survive.
The children had to survive by begging and stealing.

senses.text: to continue to live or exist despite a dangerous event or time
examples: Her 5-year-old son miraculously survived the crash.
He only survived the attack because he was wearing body armour.
Many birds didn't survive the severe winter.
The company managed to survive the crisis.
Few buildings survived the war intact.

senses.text: the process of making air, water, soil, etc. dirty; the state of being dirty
examples: air/water pollution
to reduce pollution levels
new pollution control technologies

senses.text: substances that make air, water, soil, etc. dirty
examples: beaches covered with pollution
The new buses emit no more pollution than the average car.

senses.text: permission to do something, especially given by somebody in authority
examples: The written consent of a parent is required.
Children under 16 cannot give consent to medical treatment.
to refuse/withhold your consent
He is charged with taking a car without the owner's consent.

senses.text: agreement about something
examples: She was chosen as leader by common consent (= everyone agreed to the choice).
By mutual consent they didn't go out (= they both agreed not to).

senses.text: relaxed and friendly; not following strict rules of how to behave or do something
examples: an informal atmosphere
an informal meeting/gathering/visit
an informal arrangement
informal discussions/talks
an informal chat/conversation

senses.text: suitable for normal conversation and writing to friends rather than for serious speech and letters
examples: an informal expression

senses.text: suitable for wearing at home or when relaxing rather than for a special or an official occasion
examples: I changed into more informal clothes.
Dress is informal, and storage space often limited, so you’ll be more comfortable travelling light.

senses.text: an official action or decision that has happened in the past and that is seen as an example or a rule to be followed in a similar situation later
examples: The ruling set a precedent for future libel cases.

senses.text: a similar action or event that happened earlier
examples: historical precedents
There is no precedent for a disaster of this scale.
Such protests are without precedent in recent history.
The achievements of this period were without precedent in history.

senses.text: a set of rooms for living in, usually on one floor of a building
examples: an apartment building
I was renting a two-bedroom apartment in an old building.
A group of developers wants to build a high-rise apartment complex.
I lived on the ground floor of an apartment block.
They left their fourth-floor apartment on Mott Street and walked to the bus.

senses.text: a set of rooms used for a holiday
examples: self-catering holiday apartments
They are staying in a luxury apartment overlooking the beach.

senses.text: something that happens or exists
examples: a common/everyday/frequent/regular occurrence
Vandalism used to be a rare occurrence here.
The program counts the number of occurrences of any word within the text.

senses.text: the fact of something happening or existing
examples: the recent occurrence of skin cancer in younger patients

senses.text: angry, and behaving in a threatening way; ready to attack
examples: Seals have been known to exhibit aggressive behaviour towards swimmers.
They became increasingly aggressive as the evening wore on.
There's no need to get aggressive.
She warned that her dog was aggressive towards strangers.

senses.text: determined and acting with force in order to succeed
examples: a very aggressive advertising campaign
We need to get more aggressive in our approach.
The firm is extremely aggressive in seeking new markets.

senses.text: your own; not belonging to or connected with anyone else
examples: The novel is written from personal experience.
a personal account of her experiences
personal effects/belongings/possessions
I take personal responsibility for the incident.
Of course, this is just a personal opinion.

senses.text: between individual people who know each other
examples: Having good personal relationships is the most important thing for me.
She is a personal friend of mine.

senses.text: unhappy because you have no friends or people to talk to
examples: She lives alone and often feels lonely.

senses.text: sad and spent alone
examples: all those lonely nights at home watching TV
a lonely childhood
She contemplated the lonely walk home.
These characters live sad, lonely lives.

senses.text: where only a few people ever come or visit
examples: a lonely beach
a lonely mountain road
It gets pretty lonely here in winter.

senses.text: an organization for helping people in need
examples: a registered charity
the UK's largest children's charity
Many charities sent money to help the victims of the famine.
The concert will raise money for local charities.
My parents always supported charities.

senses.text: organizations for helping people in need, considered as a group; the money, food, help, etc. that they give
examples: Most of the runners in the London Marathon are raising money for charity.
A portion of the proceeds was donated to charity.
Do you give much to charity?
a charity event (= organized to get money for charity)
All the profits go to charity.

senses.text: to show or suggest that somebody is involved in something bad or criminal
examples: He tried to avoid saying anything that would implicate him further.

senses.text: to show or suggest that something is the cause of something bad
examples: The results implicate poor hygiene as one cause of the outbreak.

senses.text: to build or put something together again
examples: After the earthquake, the people set about rebuilding their homes.
He rebuilt the engine using parts from cars that had been scrapped.
It could take ten years for the area to completely rebuild after the hurricane.

senses.text: to make something/somebody complete and strong again
examples: When she lost her job, she had to rebuild her life completely.
attempts to rebuild the shattered post-war economy
After the divorce, it took me a long time to rebuild my confidence.

senses.text: a sentence, phrase or word that asks for information
examples: You didn't answer my question.
You will have the opportunity to ask questions.
Does anyone have any questions?
I would now like to pose two questions:…
That's a very interesting question.

senses.text: a task or request for information that is intended to test your knowledge or understanding, for example in an exam or a competition
examples: Question 3 was very difficult.
I couldn't do Question 6.
You must answer all ten questions.
In the exam there's sure to be a question on energy.

senses.text: a person who has recently joined the armed forces or the police
examples: the training of new recruits
He spoke of us scornfully as raw recruits (= people without training or experience).

senses.text: a person who joins an organization, a company, etc.
examples: They are stepping up attempts to attract new recruits to the nursing profession.

senses.text: to work together in order to achieve a particular aim; to organize a group of people to do this
examples: The unions mobilized thousands of workers in a protest against the cuts.
They successfully mobilized public opinion against him.

senses.text: to find and start to use something that is needed for a particular purpose
examples: They were unable to mobilize the resources they needed.

senses.text: if a country mobilizes its army, or if a country or army mobilizes, it makes itself ready to fight in a war
examples: The troops were ordered to mobilize.
The military is able to mobilize rapidly.

senses.text: opposed to great or sudden social change; showing that you prefer traditional styles and values
examples: the conservative views of his parents
music which is accessible to an audience with extremely conservative tastes
The southern state's inhabitants tend to be socially conservative.
Her style of dress was never conservative.
They were deeply conservative in their outlook.

senses.text: connected with the British Conservative Party
examples: Conservative members/supporters

senses.text: to feel sorry about something you have done or about something that you have not been able to do
examples: If you don't do it now, you'll only regret it.
The decision could be one he lives to regret.
I soon came to regret my action.
‘I've had a wonderful life,’ she said, ‘I don't regret a thing.’
She regretted the words the moment they were out of her mouth.

senses.text: used to say in a polite or formal way that you are sorry or sad about a situation
examples: The airline regrets any inconvenience.
The president said that his country deeply regretted the incident.
The United States regrets the loss of innocent civilian lives.
I regret that I am unable to accept your kind invitation.
I very much regret that I cannot be with you this evening.

senses.text: the middle point or part of something
examples: He walked to the centre of the circle.
There was a long table in the centre of the room.
The statue is in the very centre of the temple.
The brownie should be firm on the outside but soft in the centre.
Aristotle believed that the Earth was at the centre of the universe.

senses.text: the main part of a town or city where there are a lot of shops and offices
examples: I got a lift into the centre of town.
a town-centre car park

senses.text: an animal or a thing that is very large or ugly
examples: Their dog's an absolute monster!

senses.text: a person who is very cruel and evil
examples: The man is a monster; he terrifies me.
What sort of inhuman monster could do such a thing?
The tabloid papers labelled him ‘an evil sex monster’.

senses.text: a ball of air or gas in a liquid, or a ball of air inside a solid substance such as glass
examples: champagne bubbles
a bubble of oxygen
He blew bubbles into the water through a straw.

senses.text: a round ball of liquid, containing air, produced by soap and water
examples: The children like to have bubbles in their bath.
The twins were happily blowing bubbles.
They jumped about, bursting the bubbles.

senses.text: a feeling that somebody has done something wrong, illegal or dishonest, even though you have no proof
examples: They drove away slowly to avoid arousing suspicion.
He was arrested on suspicion of murder.
I have a sneaking suspicion that she's not telling the truth.
My suspicions were confirmed when police raided the property.
She was reluctant to voice her suspicions.

senses.text: a feeling or belief that something is true, even though you have no proof
examples: I have a horrible suspicion that we've come to the wrong station.

senses.text: the feeling that you cannot trust somebody/something
examples: Their offer was greeted with some suspicion.
Water from shallow wells should be regarded with suspicion, as it may be contaminated.

senses.text: freedom to live as you choose without too many limits from government or authority
examples: the fight for justice and liberty
The concept of individual liberty is enshrined in the constitution.

senses.text: the state of not being a prisoner or a slave
examples: He had to endure six months' loss of liberty.

senses.text: to look quickly at something/somebody
examples: She glanced at her watch.
He glanced around the room.
I glanced up quickly to see who had come in.

senses.text: to read something quickly and not carefully or completely
examples: I only had time to glance at my emails.
He glanced briefly down the list of names.
She glanced through the report.

senses.text: a place where you can buy and drink alcoholic and other drinks
examples: We arranged to meet in a bar called the Flamingo.
The area is full of restaurants and bars.
The bar staff are very friendly.
It's the island’s only licensed bar (= one that is allowed to sell alcoholic drinks).
I found David in the bar of the Red Lion (= a room in a pub where drinks are served).

senses.text: a place in which a particular kind of food or drink is the main thing that is served
examples: a sushi bar
The hotel has a cocktail bar on the top floor.

senses.text: a complete lack of noise or sound
examples: Their footsteps echoed in the silence.
A scream broke the silence of the night.
I need absolute silence when I'm working.

senses.text: a situation when nobody is speaking
examples: an embarrassed/awkward/uncomfortable silence
a moment’s stunned silence
I got used to his long silences.
They finished their meal in total silence.
She lapsed into silence again.

senses.text: to produce or reflect light; to be bright
examples: The sun shone brightly in a cloudless sky.
A light was shining in the distance.
The dark polished wood shone like glass.
The stars were shining and the moon was bright.
The hot desert sun shone down relentlessly.

senses.text: to aim or point the light of a lamp, etc. in a particular direction
examples: He shone the flashlight around the cellar.
The watchman shone his torch at us.
The doctor shone the light in my eyes several times.
Campaigners are shining a spotlight on the world's diminishing natural resources.

senses.text: the part of a door, window, etc. that you use to open it
examples: She turned the handle and opened the door.
a door handle

senses.text: the part of an object, such as a cup, a bag or a tool, that you use to hold it or carry it
examples: the handle of a knife
a broom handle
The handle's broken off this jug.

senses.text: the ability to create pictures in your mind; the part of your mind that does this
examples: a vivid/fertile imagination
He has no imagination.
It doesn't take much imagination to guess what she meant.
I won't tell you his reaction—I'll leave that to your imagination.
Don't let your imagination run away with you (= don't use too much imagination).

senses.text: something that you have imagined rather than something that exists
examples: She was no longer able to distinguish between imagination and reality.
Is it my imagination or have you lost a lot of weight?

senses.text: the ability to have new and exciting ideas
examples: His writing lacks imagination.
With a little imagination, you could turn this place into a palace.
We are looking for someone with ingenuity and imagination.

senses.text: a person who reads, especially one who reads a lot or in a particular way
examples: an avid reader of science fiction
a fast/slow reader
And so, dear reader, our tale comes to its end.
The reader is left to draw his or her own conclusions.
There are many examples to help the reader understand the usefulness of this approach.

senses.text: a person who reads a particular newspaper, magazine, etc.
examples: readers’ letters
Are you a ‘Times’ reader?
regular readers of this magazine
Readers of this blog will know my views on the matter.
Alert readers may have noticed the misprint in last week's column.

senses.text: having enough skill or knowledge to do something well or to the necessary standard
examples: He's very competent in his work.
Make sure the firm is competent to carry out the work.
I don’t feel competent to comment.
She is highly competent at her job.

senses.text: of a good standard but not very good
examples: Ron was a competent player—more than that, he was good!

senses.text: a person who is trying to be elected or is applying for a job
examples: a presidential candidate
one of the leading candidates for the presidency
He is the best candidate for the job.
She stood as a candidate in the local elections.
The party is fielding more candidates than ever before.

senses.text: a person taking an exam
examples: Candidates are allowed to use dictionaries in this examination.
We run special revision courses for examination candidates.
a candidate for the degree of MPhil

senses.text: to mix with a liquid and become part of it
examples: Salt dissolves in water.
Heat gently until the sugar dissolves.
The aspirin hasn't dissolved completely yet.

senses.text: to make a solid become part of a liquid
examples: Dissolve the tablet in water.

senses.text: to express the meaning of speech or writing in a different language
examples: He translated the letter into English.
Her books have been translated into 24 languages.
Can you help me translate this legal jargon into plain English?
This chapter was translated from the French by Oliver Breen.
His works have been translated from French into countless languages.

senses.text: to be changed from one language to another
examples: Most poetry does not translate well.
The Welsh name translates as ‘Land's End’.
It's a Spanish word that roughly translates as 'unease'.
‘Tiramisu’ literally translates as ‘pull-me-up’.

senses.text: to move slowly on water or in the air
examples: A group of swans floated by.
The smell of new bread floated up from the kitchen.
Beautiful music came floating out of the window.
The boats were floating gently down the river.
Add the gnocchi and cook until they float to the surface.

senses.text: to stay on or near the surface of a liquid and not sink
examples: Wood floats.
Much of the bay is covered by floating sea ice.
She relaxed, floating gently in the water.
A plastic bag was floating on the water.

senses.text: connected with religion or with a particular religion
examples: religious beliefs/convictions/faith
religious freedom/liberty
religious traditions/practices/ceremonies
religious institutions/organizations
religious education (= education about religion)

senses.text: believing strongly in a particular religion and obeying its laws and practices
examples: His wife is very religious.
a deeply religious man
Although I'm not particularly religious, I whispered a few words of prayer.
His mother noticed that he had become more religious.

senses.text: an area of knowledge or activity; especially one that somebody is responsible for
examples: Financial matters are her domain.
Physics used to be very much a male domain.
things that happen outside the domain of the home

senses.text: a set of websites on the internet that end with the same group of letters, for example ‘.com’, ‘.org’
examples: top-level domains

senses.text: to deliberately not include something in what you are doing or considering
examples: The cost of borrowing has been excluded from the inflation figures.
Try excluding fat from your diet.
Buses run every hour, Sundays excluded.

senses.text: to prevent somebody/something from entering a place or taking part in something
examples: Women are still excluded from some London clubs.
Concern is growing over the number of children excluded from school (= not allowed to attend because of bad behaviour).
She felt excluded by the other girls (= they did not let her join in what they were doing).

senses.text: to decide that something is not possible
examples: We should not exclude the possibility of negotiation.
The police have excluded theft as a motive for the murder.
The possibility of error cannot be absolutely excluded.

senses.text: willing to use their power to do dishonest or illegal things in return for money or to get an advantage
examples: It was seen as the only way to overthrow a corrupt regime.
They promised to seek out the corrupt officials who had accepted the bribes.
one of the most notoriously corrupt city councils

senses.text: not honest or moral
examples: They had been engaged in corrupt practices.
The whole system is inefficient and corrupt.

senses.text: to control or influence somebody/something, often in a dishonest way so that they do not realize it
examples: She uses her charm to manipulate people.
As a politician, he knows how to manipulate public opinion.
They managed to manipulate us into agreeing to help.

senses.text: to control, use or change something with skill
examples: to manipulate the gears and levers of a machine
Computers are very efficient at manipulating information.
genetically manipulated organisms
digitally manipulated images

senses.text: to question whether something is true or legally or officially acceptable
examples: These figures have been disputed.
to dispute a decision/claim
The family wanted to dispute the will.
No one is disputing that there is a problem.
It is disputed whether the law applies in this case.

senses.text: to argue or disagree strongly with somebody about something, especially about who owns something
examples: I tried to dispute the bill with the waiter.
The United Nations recognizes the area as a disputed territory.
The issue remains hotly disputed.
He taught and disputed with local poets.

senses.text: a female child
examples: a little girl of six
a six-year-old girl
a baby/young/teenage girl
a beautiful/clever girl
When I was a girl, I wanted to be a dancer.

senses.text: a daughter
examples: They have two girls and a boy.
Our youngest girl is at college.

senses.text: a young woman
examples: He married the girl next door.
It's a great way to meet girls.
Alex is not interested in girls yet.

senses.text: the tools or other pieces of equipment that are needed for a particular activity or task
examples: a piece of laboratory apparatus
Firefighters needed breathing apparatus to enter the burning house.
Science departments say they are lacking basic apparatus.

senses.text: the structure of a system or an organization, particularly that of a political party or a government
examples: the power of the state apparatus
We cannot do without the apparatus of government in protecting the rights of the individual.

senses.text: a large official meeting, usually lasting for a few days, at which people with the same work or interests come together to discuss their views
examples: The hotel is used for exhibitions, conferences and social events.
to host/organize a conference
to speak at/address a conference
The conference will be held in Glasgow.
Delegates to the Labour Party's annual conference will be staying in the Grand Hotel.

senses.text: a meeting at which people have formal discussions
examples: They agreed to convene a peace conference by mid-November.
Her working day consists of conferences with foreign diplomats.
He was in conference with his lawyers all day.
Ministers from all four countries involved will meet at the conference table this week.

senses.text: clear and easy to understand, so that you have no doubt what is meant
examples: He gave me very explicit directions on how to get there.
The reasons for the decision should be made explicit.
She made some very explicit references to my personal life.

senses.text: saying something clearly, exactly and openly
examples: She was quite explicit about why she had left.

senses.text: to demand that something happen or that somebody agree to do something
examples: I didn't really want to go but he insisted.
Stay and have lunch. I insist!
‘Please come with us.’ ‘Very well then, if you insist.’
She insisted on him wearing a suit.
She insisted on his wearing a suit.

senses.text: to state clearly that something is true, especially when other people do not believe you
examples: He insisted on his innocence.
He insisted (that) he was innocent.
The government has always insisted that this was not the case.
‘It's true,’ she insisted.

senses.text: to show that something is true or exists
examples: Initial tests indicate the presence of oxygen.
The survey data indicate a general satisfaction with the process.
Although the president promised to foster unity, his actions have indicated otherwise.
Research indicates that eating habits are changing fast.
These findings clearly indicate that the scheme has improved pedestrian safety.

senses.text: to be a sign of something; to show that something is possible or likely
examples: A red sky at night often indicates fine weather the next day.
Early results indicate that the government will be returned to power.
These figures indicate to me that the company is in serious trouble.

senses.text: a relationship between friends
examples: They formed a close friendship at college.
a lasting/lifelong friendship
to forge/develop/cultivate a friendship
He seemed to have already struck up (= begun) a friendship with Jo.
It's the story of an extraordinary friendship between a boy and a seal.

senses.text: the feeling or relationship that friends have; the state of being friends
examples: Your friendship is very important to me.
a conference to promote international friendship
It was largely out of friendship for him that I agreed to come to Turin.

senses.text: to introduce a new law, rule, tax, etc.; to order that a rule, punishment, etc. be used
examples: The UN Security Council imposed sanctions in 1992.
to impose a penalty/fine/sentence/ban
A new tax was imposed on fuel.

senses.text: to force somebody/something to have to deal with something that is difficult or unpleasant
examples: He described the limitations imposed by his disease.
This system imposes additional financial burdens on many people.
to impose restrictions/constraints/obligations on somebody/something
The time limits are imposed on us by factors outside our control.

senses.text: the state of not having enough food to eat, especially when this causes illness or death
examples: Around fifty people die of hunger every day in the camp.
The organization works to alleviate world hunger and disease.

senses.text: the feeling caused by a need to eat
examples: If you feel hunger pangs between meals, eat some fruit or nuts.
I felt faint with hunger.

senses.text: the power to give orders to people
examples: in a position of authority
to undermine/challenge somebody's authority
She now has authority over the people who used to be her bosses.
The law states that parents exercise authority over their children.
Nothing will be done because no one in authority (= who has a position of power) takes the matter seriously.

senses.text: the power or right to do something
examples: Some experts think the agency may have exceeded its authority.
The government has the authority to regulate the economy.
The Constitution gives Congress the authority to decide whether to fund military action.
We have the authority to search this building.

senses.text: a device that keeps a door, window, box, etc. shut, usually needing a key to open it
examples: She turned the key in the lock.
It's a good idea to have locks fitted on all your windows.

senses.text: a device that prevents a vehicle, machine or piece of equipment from being used
examples: a bicycle lock
a steering lock
safety locks for handguns

senses.text: the detailed study or examination of something in order to understand more about it; the result of the study
examples: statistical/data analysis
a detailed analysis of the data
to perform/conduct an analysis
Further analysis revealed significant regional variations in the results.
We made the decision based on our analysis of the situation.

senses.text: a careful examination of a substance in order to find out what it consists of
examples: The blood samples are sent to the laboratory for analysis.
the results of a DNA sequence analysis
You can ask for a chemical analysis of your tap water.
